title: Hierarchical Grouping
concept: Hierarchical grouping is the bottom-up process of iteratively merging small pixel regions into larger candidate boxes until the entire image is merged.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Is Selective Search a neural network algorithm?

options.text: No, it is a classical CPU-based algorithm with fixed rules
Yes, it requires training on GPUs

exercises.explain: Selective Search is a classical algorithm that relies on hand-crafted pixel rules.

cards.title: Zooming Out
cards.body: If you look closely at a digital photo, you see individual pixels. Zoom out a bit, and they form a leaf. Zoom out more, and they form a tree branch. Zoom out all the way, and you see the entire forest. This bottom-up hierarchy is how grouping builds object boundaries.

cards.title: Hierarchical Grouping
cards.body: Hierarchical Grouping is the bottom-up pipeline where Selective Search starts with thousands of small, over-segmented regions. In each iteration, it merges the two most similar adjacent regions, recording the bounding box of the merged shape at each step.

cards.title: Streams to Oceans
cards.body: Think of tiny rain droplets forming small streams. The streams flow together to form rivers, and the rivers merge to form a single massive ocean. The hierarchy captures every level of structure along the way.

cards.title: The Proposal Tree
cards.body: Selective Search doesn't just output the final merged image. It outputs the bounding boxes of every intermediate region created during the merging process. This typically yields about 2,000 candidate boxes per image.

cards.title: Not infinite regions
cards.body: You might think merging every step would create an infinite loop or millions of boxes. In reality, it stops when the entire image is merged into a single region, keeping the total candidates capped at a manageable number (like 2,000).

cards.title: Bottom-Up Hierarchy
cards.body: Visualizing the levels of hierarchical region merging:

exercises.prompt: What does Selective Search output as region proposals?
exercises.explain: Selective Search records and outputs bounding boxes for every intermediate merged shape throughout the hierarchy, catching objects at multiple scales.

options.text: The bounding boxes of all intermediate merged regions in the hierarchy
Only the final box containing the whole image
A set of random crop coordinates
The class labels predicted by the backbone

exercises.prompt: True or False: Hierarchical grouping starts with large candidate boxes and cuts them into smaller pieces.
exercises.explain: False. It is a bottom-up algorithm: it starts with tiny pixel segments and merges them into larger regions.
The process moves bottom-up, growing regions by merging smaller neighbors.
exercises.sentence: Hierarchical grouping is a ___ process, starting with pixels and ending with the whole image.

exercises.prompt: Which output size represents the typical number of proposals from Selective Search?
exercises.explain: Selective Search is typically configured to generate around 2,000 candidate proposals per image.

exercises.prompt: Why is hierarchical merging crucial for detecting both a 'wheel' and the 'car' it belongs to in the same image?

options.text: Early steps in the hierarchy capture small parts (like the wheel); later steps capture the whole object (the car). Both are recorded.
Because the wheel and the car are the same color.
To reduce the feature map size by half.
It isn't; the model only cares about the largest object.

exercises.explain: Exactly! By saving bounding boxes at all levels of the merge hierarchy, the model can propose candidate locations for nested objects (wheels, windows) and macro objects (cars, buildings) simultaneously. Next: let's look at the speed performance of this classical method!
